criteria: The agent has read Section 3.1 completely to understand the QAT-LoRA baseline and its three key limitations: (1) weight gradients still required for quantization parameter updates, (2) mixed-precision model output, and (3) decoupled gradient updates for quantization and LoRA parameters.

The agent has read Section 3.2 completely to understand the three core design choices of L4Q: fully-quantized linear layer (combining weights before quantization), memory-efficient QAT (local weight gradient computation and flushing), and efficient LoRA training (reusing weight gradients for LoRA parameter updates).

The agent has read Section 3.3 and Figure 3 to understand the L4Q quantization parameter initialization scheme (L4Qinit) and how it differs from LSQ+, symmetric, and asymmetric initialization methods.

The agent has read Section 4.1 to understand all experimental settings, including target models (OpenLLaMA 3B, LLaMA-1 7B/13B/33B, LLaMA-2 7B/13B, LLaMA-3 8B, Mistral-v0.1 7B), baselines (LSQ, GPTQ, OmniQuant, QLoRA, QA-LoRA, LoftQ, QAT-LoRA), dataset (Stanford-Alpaca), and evaluation benchmarks (CSQA and MMLU).
The agent has read Table 1 and Figure 4 to understand the memory cost comparison and inference speedup results that L4Q should achieve.
The agent has read Tables 2 and 3 to understand the expected 4-bit and 3-bit accuracy results for all models and baselines.

The plan includes implementing the L4Q fully-quantized linear layer that combines base weights and LoRA parameters before quantization (Equation 7: Wcomb = W0 + αBA), then applies quantization to the combined weight matrix.
The plan includes the memory-efficient QAT strategy of locally computing weight gradients (∂L/∂Wq) and immediately flushing them after computing quantization parameter gradients.

The plan includes implementing efficient LoRA training by reusing the weight gradients computed for QAT parameter updates when computing LoRA parameter gradients, with STE and conditional clamping applied.
The plan includes the L4Q quantization parameter initialization (L4Qinit): a symmetric scheme using s = Max(|Min(W)/Qn|, |Max(W)/Qp|) with bias b = 0.
The plan includes implementing all baselines: LSQ (QAT), GPTQ, OmniQuant (PTQ methods), and QLoRA, QA-LoRA, LoftQ, QAT-LoRA (quantization-aware PEFT methods).

The plan includes the correct training hyperparameters: AdamW optimizer with weight decay 0.01, cosine decay LR scheduler with 10% linear warm-up, batch size 128, 25K training iterations for QAT-based methods (L4Q, QAT-LoRA) and 50K iterations for PTQ-based methods (QLoRA, QA-LoRA), and bfloat16 precision.
The plan includes model-specific quantization group sizes: group size 128 for LLaMA and Mistral models, and group size 64 for OpenLLaMA models.
The plan includes LoRA rank configuration: default rank r=4 for most models and r=8 for Mistral; and QA-LoRA uses doubled rank (r=8) for fair comparison in terms of LoRA parameter count.

The code correctly implements the L4Q fully-quantized linear layer forward pass: first computes Wcomb = W0 + αBA (Equation 7), then quantizes Wcomb to obtain the integer representation w_tilde and dequantized weight Wq (Equations 8-9), and finally computes the output as Y = Wq * X (Equation 10).

The code correctly implements the uniform quantization function with round and clamp operations: w_tilde = round(clamp((W - b)/s, Qn, Qp)) where Qn = -2^(n-1) and Qp = 2^(n-1) - 1, and the dequantized weight Wq = w_tilde * s + b.

The code implements memory-efficient QAT by computing the weight gradient locally as ∂L/∂Wq = ∂L/∂Y * X^T (Equation 11) and immediately discarding it after using it to compute quantization parameter gradients, avoiding persistent storage of the weight gradient tensor.

The code correctly implements the quantization scale (s) gradient computation using the STE: ∂Wq/∂s = (-w + w_tilde) if Qn ≤ w ≤ Qp, and Qn or Qp at the clipping boundaries (Equation 5/19), and the bias (b) gradient: ∂Wq/∂b = 0 if Qn ≤ w ≤ Qp, and 1 otherwise (Equation 6/21).

The code correctly implements LoRA parameter gradient computation by reusing the pre-computed weight gradient ∂L/∂Wq: computes ∂Wq/∂A = αB^T if Qn ≤ w ≤ Qp, else 0 (Equation 13), and ∂Wq/∂B = αA^T if Qn ≤ w ≤ Qp, else 0 (Equation 14), then multiplies with ∂L/∂Wq (Equation 12).

The code correctly implements the L4Q initialization (L4Qinit): sets scale s = Max(|Min(W)/Qn|, |Max(W)/Qp|) with bias b = 0, which captures both minimum and maximum outliers conservatively (Equation 15/37-38).

The code correctly implements the QAT-LoRA baseline, which keeps pre-trained weights frozen, adds LoRA parameters to linear layers, and introduces quantization scales and bias parameters trained using QAT (LSQ-style), but quantizes and uses LoRA parameters separately (producing a mixed-precision model).
The code correctly initializes LoRA parameters: matrix A is initialized with Kaiming-uniform distribution and matrix B is initialized to zeros.

The code loads and processes the Stanford-Alpaca dataset (50K training samples, 2K validation samples) and correctly sets the training sequence length to 2048 (with the exception of LLaMA-1 33B with L4Q which uses 128).

The code correctly configures quantization group size: 128 for LLaMA and Mistral models, 64 for OpenLLaMA models, applying group-wise quantization where weight elements within each group share the same quantization scale s and zero-point b.
The code sets LoRA rank r=4 by default (r=8 for Mistral-v0.1 7B), and uses doubled rank for QA-LoRA (r=8 instead of 4) to ensure a fair comparison in terms of number of LoRA parameters.

The code correctly implements the AdamW optimizer with weight decay 0.01 and a cosine decay learning rate scheduler with linear warm-up over 10% of total training steps, with model-specific learning rates as listed in Table 9 (Appendix D).

The code correctly sets training iterations: 25K iterations for QAT-based methods (QAT, QAT-LoRA, L4Q) and 50K iterations for PTQ-based PEFT methods (QLoRA, QA-LoRA), with batch size 128 and bfloat16 precision.

The code implements evaluation on the Commonsense QA (CSQA) benchmark comprising seven multiple-choice tasks: HellaSwag, PIQA, ARC-c, ARC-e, Winogrande, BoolQ, and OpenBookQA, and reports average accuracy.
The code implements evaluation on the MMLU benchmark covering four subject categories (Humanities, STEM, Social Sciences, Others) with both 0-shot and 5-shot settings, and reports average accuracy.
The memory cost measurement experiment for 4-bit fine-tuning is executed without errors on OpenLLaMA 3B, LLaMA-1 7B, 13B, and 33B models, comparing LoRA, QAT, QAT-LoRA, and L4Q.

The inference speedup experiment is executed without errors using LLaMA-1 7B, 13B, and 33B models, measuring throughput (tokens/sec) at batch sizes 1 to 64 for fully-quantized models (L4Q) vs. mixed-precision models (QLoRA) vs. fp16 baselines.

The 4-bit quantization accuracy experiments are executed successfully for all models (OpenLLaMA 3B, LLaMA-1 7B/13B/33B, LLaMA-2 7B/13B, LLaMA-3 8B, Mistral-v0.1 7B) and all methods (GPTQ, OmniQ, LoftQ, QLoRA, QA-LoRA, QAT-LoRA, L4Q) on both CSQA and MMLU benchmarks.
The 3-bit quantization accuracy experiments are executed successfully for all models and all methods (GPTQ, OmniQ, LoftQ, QLoRA, QA-LoRA, QAT-LoRA, L4Q) on both CSQA and MMLU benchmarks.

The executed memory cost results match Table 1: L4Q achieves memory cost comparable to LoRA (e.g., ~15.3 GB for OpenLLaMA 3B vs 15.1 GB for LoRA), while QAT and QAT-LoRA incur significantly higher memory overhead (44.2 GB and 22.6 GB respectively for 3B).

The executed inference speedup results match Figure 4 and Table 12: fully-quantized 4-bit models (L4Q) achieve 1.8x–2.3x speedup over fp16 baselines, while mixed-precision models (LoftQ, QLoRA, QAT-LoRA) achieve only 1.33x–1.44x speedup, with fully-quantized models achieving 30%–50% greater speedup than mixed-precision counterparts.

The 4-bit accuracy results match Table 2: L4Q achieves the highest accuracy among all methods on both CSQA and MMLU benchmarks across all models, outperforming QAT-LoRA (which also uses QAT but keeps quantization and LoRA decoupled) and all PTQ-based PEFT methods.

The 3-bit accuracy results match Table 3: L4Q demonstrates even more pronounced advantages over baselines at 3-bit quantization compared to 4-bit, with some PTQ-based PEFT approaches (e.g., LoftQ at 3-bit) experiencing significant accuracy degradation, while L4Q maintains substantially higher accuracy.

The quantization initialization ablation results match Table 4 in Appendix B: L4Qinit achieves the lowest clipping error (36.1×10^3 post-training) and the highest MMLU 5-shot accuracy (45.3%) on LLaMA-2 7B at 4-bit, compared to LSQ+init (360.6×10^3, 25.7%), Symm (282.1×10^3, 43.5%), and Asymm (64.7×10^3, 44.2%).

The LoRA rank ablation results match Tables 5 and 6 in Appendix C: increasing LoRA rank beyond 4 yields negligible performance improvement on LLaMA-2 7B CSQA and MMLU benchmarks, validating the choice of rank r=4 as default.

The quantization group size ablation results match Tables 7 and 8 in Appendix C: smaller group sizes (32 or 64) yield slightly higher accuracy than group size 128 on LLaMA-2 7B, validating the choice of group size 128 as a trade-off between accuracy and overhead.
